The `CoverImage` component uses `ExportedImage` (next-image-export-optimizer), which in turn uses Next.js `Image`. When `fill` is used, Next.js may add a `<link rel="preload">` tag in the `<head>` for images that are "above the fold" (i.e., loaded eagerly without `loading="lazy"`).

The issue is: the Unsplash image URL `https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1466692476868-aef1dfb1e735?auto=format&fit=crop&w=1200&q=80` is in the markdown content of `content/series/markdown-showcase/visuals-and-diagrams.mdx`, but it's appearing as a preloaded resource on the books page.

---
title: "Visuals and Diagrams"
date: "2026-02-13"
category: "Design"
tags: ["visuals", "mermaid"]
---

Visualizing information is key to understanding. Amytis integrates Mermaid.js for diagrams and handles images with ease.

## Mermaid Diagrams

You can write diagrams directly in your markdown:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Idea] --> B{Is it good?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Plant it]
    B -- No --> D[Discard]
    C --> E[Grow]
    E --> F[Evergreen]
```

## Images

Standard markdown images are supported, including relative paths:

![Digital Garden](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1466692476868-aef1dfb1e735?auto=format&fit=crop&w=1200&q=80)

Done. A few notes on how this works:

- `error_page 404 /404.html` is placed at the `server {}` level so it applies to all 404s site-wide
- When `try_files $uri $uri/index.html =404` hits the `=404` fallback, Nginx does an internal redirect to `/404.html`, which exists at the root of the static export

Here's the full picture:

**Why 404s happen:** `getAllTags()` normalizes all tags to lowercase when building the tag index, so `generateStaticParams()` only generates static pages for lowercase slugs (e.g., `/tags/javascript`). **My recommendation: yes, make URLs consistently lowercase** — but only at the URL level, not in the display text. Lowercasing tag names like `JavaScript` or `TypeScript` in display would be wrong; they're proper nouns. The data layer (`getPostsByTag`, `getAllTags`) already does case-insensitive matching correctly. The fix is small: add `.toLowerCase()` to the two places that are missing it.

The alternative — normalizing tags to lowercase at parse time in `markdown.ts` — would break display text and is more invasive for no benefit.
